name: Division Utility
summary: This module provides the core business logic for dividing two floating-point numbers (a / b). It implements robust error handling by explicitly raising a descriptive `ZeroDivisionError` when the divisor is zero. Furthermore, it includes specific logic to detect and warn about near-zero divisors (those whose absolute value falls below a defined threshold), addressing an intentional precision-loss edge case.
responsibilities: Perform floating-point division of two numbers.
Handle the explicit error case where the divisor is zero by raising a descriptive `ZeroDivisionError`.
Issue a runtime warning when the divisor is extremely small (near zero), alerting the user to potential precision loss.

public_interfaces.name: divide
public_interfaces.description: Divides the first argument (a) by the second argument (b). It handles zero division and near-zero divisors gracefully.

citations.note: Defines the core division logic and error handling.

paths.title: Zero Division Error Handling (Red Path)
paths.narrative: If the divisor `b` is exactly zero, the function immediately raises a custom `ZeroDivisionError`. This provides a specific and descriptive error message (`cannot divide {a} by zero}`) instead of relying on Python's default division error.

citations.note: The explicit check `if b == 0:` and subsequent raise statement.

paths.title: Near-Zero Divisor Warning (Yellow Path)
paths.narrative: If the absolute value of the divisor `b` is less than the defined threshold (`PRECISION_WARNING_THRESHOLD`, which is 1e-6), a warning is issued using `warnings.warn()`. This alerts the user that the resulting division may lose floating-point precision, even though the calculation proceeds.

citations.note: The check `if abs(b) < PRECISION_WARNING_THRESHOLD:` and subsequent warning call.

paths.title: Standard Division (Green Path)
paths.narrative: When the divisor `b` is non-zero and sufficiently far from zero, the function executes standard floating-point division (`a / b`) and returns the result.

citations.note: The final return statement `return a / b`.
